coredata.dc:title: A simple label-free photoelectrochemical aptasensor for ultrasensitive detection of thrombin
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Chaonan
Li, Zhiyuan
Tian, Ya
Guo, Qingfu
Nie, Guangming
coredata.dc:description: This work demonstrates a label-free photoelectrochemical (PEC) aptamer sensor based on poly(5-formylindole)/Au (P5FIn/Au) nanocomposites for the ultrasensitive determination of thrombin (TB). As a photosensitive material, P5FIn exhibits excellent photocurrent response under visible light irradiation, and can capture more thrombin aptamers (TBA) due to large specific surface area and surface-rich aldehyde group. In addition, the aldehyde groups of P5FIn are conducive to the stable and efficient immobilization of aptamers or bioactive molecules, enhancing their biological activity. Au increases the absorption of visible light and effectively inhibits the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby improving the photocurrent conversion efficiency of P5FIn. Therefore, after TB is specifically identified, the photocurrent decreases linearly. Under optimal conditions, this PEC aptasensor has a linear response ranging from 0.001 to 10 nM with detection limits as low as 0.2 pM. This sensor also has good selectivity, repeatability, stability and simple construction process. The determination results of TB in real human serum samples are also satisfactory, which will provide a practical PEC platform for the rapid detection of proteins in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemistry
Aptasensor
Poly(5-formylindole)
Au
Thrombin
Nanocomposite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability, reproducibility and selectivity of the PEC aptasensor
Actual samples detection of PEC aptasensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51973102 ), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong ( ZR2019MB067 ) and Talent Fund of QUST (2019).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A) Au, (B) P5FIn, (C) P5FIn/Au. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of Au (a), P5FIn (b), P5FIn/Au (c).

(A) Photocurrent responses of the modified ITO with bare ITO (a), P5FIn (b), P5FIn/Au (c). (B) Photocurrent generation mechanism of P5FIn/Au nanocomposite. (C) EIS and (D) photocurrent responses of bare ITO (a), P5FIn/Au/ITO (b), TBA/P5FIn/Au/ITO (c), BSA/TBA/P5FIn/Au/ITO (d), TB/BSA/TBA/P5FIn/Au/ITO (e).

(A) Photocurrent responses of the aptasensor to different concentrations of TB (a) 0.001, (b) 0.005, (c) 0.01, (d) 0.05, (e) 0.1, (f) 0.5, (g) 1, (h) 5, (i) 10 nM. (B) The corresponding calibration curve of this PEC aptasensor.

(A) Time-based photocurrent responses of the aptasensor toward target TB (0.1 nM). (B) The storage stability in the presence of TB (0.01 nM) evaluated every 3 days. (C) Repeatability of five electrodes to test the different concentrations of TB (0.01 nM and 1 nM) (electrodes in the same batch and electrodes in the different batches). (D) Selective measurement of the aptasensor toward TB detection in the blank solution(a), CEA (b), HIgG (c), glucose (d), TB (0.1 nM) (e), their mixture (interfering concentration 10 nM) (f).
The construction of the PEC aptasensor based on P5FIn/Au nanocomposite.
The sensor is compared with other sensors detection for TB.
PEC aptasensor detection TB for real serum samples.

head.ce:title: A simple label-free photoelectrochemical aptasensor for ultrasensitive detection of thrombin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optic-Electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Ministry of Education, State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optic-Electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-chemical Engineering
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Optic-Electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Ministry of Education, State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work demonstrates a label-free photoelectrochemical (PEC) aptamer sensor based on poly(5-formylindole)/Au (P5FIn/Au) nanocomposites for the ultrasensitive determination of thrombin (TB). As a photosensitive material, P5FIn exhibits excellent photocurrent response under visible light irradiation, and can capture more thrombin aptamers (TBA) due to large specific surface area and surface-rich aldehyde group. In addition, the aldehyde groups of P5FIn are conducive to the stable and efficient immobilization of aptamers or bioactive molecules, enhancing their biological activity. Au increases the absorption of visible light and effectively inhibits the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby improving the photocurrent conversion efficiency of P5FIn. Therefore, after TB is specifically identified, the photocurrent decreases linearly. Under optimal conditions, this PEC aptasensor has a linear response ranging from 0.001 to 10 nM with detection limits as low as 0.2 pM. This sensor also has good selectivity, repeatability, stability and simple construction process. The determination results of TB in real human serum samples are also satisfactory, which will provide a practical PEC platform for the rapid detection of proteins in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: Thrombin (TB) is an intrinsic protein that is produced in the body at the site of vascular injury [1]. TB plays a crucial role in biogenic activities because it can selectively cleave the Arg-Gly bond in fibrinogen to form fibrin and activate platelets to clot blood [2]. Studies have shown that inactivation and overexpression of TB can produce abnormal TB and lead to many diseases, such as rheumatoid arthritis, lung cancer and hemophilia [3,4]. Thus, developing fast and sensitive methods for quantitative detecting TB is essential in the field of basic research and clinical practice. Aptamers, namely single-stranded oligonucleotides (DNA or RNA), can be specifically bound to proteins or other small molecules [5,6]. Due to design flexibility, secondary structure stability, and toilless production and storage, aptamers have been widely used to design sensors [7]. As a newly emergent analytical technique, photoelectrochemistry (PEC) has developed rapidly and attracted considerable attention in the last few years owing to short response time, low sample consumption, and small background signal. The photocurrent detection converts specific recognition reactions into corresponding photocurrent changes [8–10]. The label-free PEC aptasensor combines the advantages of optoelectronic analysis and the merits of aptamer. In addition, the label-free method not only avoids complex and time-consuming labeling processes but also refrains from repeatedly incubations compared with the probe labeling method, which effectively improves the sensitivity and specificity of the sensor [11]. Up to now, some efforts have been used to detect TB by means of PEC aptasensor platforms [12]. However, the tedious and complicated assembly processes of such sensors are still fails to meet people’s expectations for quick and easy construction of sensors, which encourages us to explore simpler and higher-performance sensing strategies.

The important basis of sensitive PEC aptasensors includes the selection and application of high-quality photoactive electrode modification materials. Conducting polymers (CPs), as optoelectronic active materials, have attracted wide attention for excellent performance of high photoelectric activity, low-toxicity, good chemical stability and electrical conductivity [13,14]. In recent years, related studies have shown that polyindole has become an emerging semiconductor material in the field of PEC analysis due to its advantages of excellent photoelectric performance, better stability, and good redox activity [15]. Interestingly, poly(5-formylindole) (P5FIn) exhibits extremely strong optical activity because the narrow band gap makes it more favorable for excitation in visible light [16]. The aldehyde groups of P5FIn are conducive to the stable and efficient immobilization of aptamers or bioactive molecules, enhancing their biological activity. CPs composited with metal nanoparticles (Co, Ag, Cu, Au et.al) have excellent chemical, optical properties and can stabilize the main components in the polymer matrix [17–20]. Among these metallic materials, Au is turned into the widest choice for biosensors construction due to excellent biocompatibility, high electrical conductivity and strong absorption of visible light [21,22], such as biosensing [23], electrochemical impedance sensing [24], surface-enhanced Raman scattering sensing [25]. More particularly, as PEC electrode materials, Au can effectively inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, so that the photocurrent conversion efficiency is improved. Based on these considerations, P5FIn/Au nanocomposite may greatly improve the photocurrent signal of the sensor and provide an ultrasensitive detection platform for TB.

In this work, a label-free PEC aptasensor was designed via P5FIn/Au nanocomposite as photosensitive materials for sensitive detection of TB. The excellent optoelectronic properties of P5FIn provides a larger loading area and more reactive sites for aptamer assembly. On the other hand, Au improved the conductivity of the modified electrode and effectively inhibited the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs of P5FIn, which lifted the photocurrent conversion efficiency. This PEC aptasensor changed the photocurrent signal by specific binding of the aptamer with TB, thereby achieving a rapid and sensitive response to TB. In actual sample testing experiment, this PEC aptasensor also showed high stability, good reproducibility and good recovery, providing a novel strategy for clinical testing and analysis of biomarkers.
The materials and instrument used were described in the Supporting Information.

Scheme 1 was the construction process of the PEC aptasensor based on P5FIn/Au nanocomposite (the preparation process can be found in Supporting Information). Firstly, 20 μL TBA (1 μM) was dropped onto the surface of the P5FIn/Au/ITO and stored at 4 °C for 12 h. TBA was modified to the electrode surface through the covalent bond between the amino group and the aldehyde group [19]. After washed by deionized water, the modified electrode was incubated with 20 μL BSA solution (0.1%) for 1 h at 4 °C to block nonspecific sites. Washed again by deionized water, the modified electrode was incubated in series concentrations of TB solution at 37 °C for 70 min, in which TB specifically was reacted with TBA on the surface of P5FIn/Au/ITO. Finally, the resulting electrode was thoroughly rinsed with deionized water and stored in 4 °C environment until testing.

PEC measurement was carried out in 0.1 M phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution (pH 7.4). During the measurement, oxygen gas was used as an electron acceptor. The application potential was 0.0 V, turning the light ON and OFF every 20 s.

Fig. 1 was the SEM images and UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of Au, P5FIn, and P5FIn/Au nanocomposite, respectively. It was worth noting that Au was electrochemically deposited on the electrode surface to form Au nanoflowers with a size of 100–200 nm (Fig. 1A). This morphology was beneficial to improve the specific surface area of the material, and the Au nanoparticle can provide more metal cores for the initial polymerization of the polymer [26]. As can be seen from Fig. 1B, P5FIn film was a rough layered structure. The morphology of the P5FIn/Au nanocomposite was dominated by the fiber grid structure, which was significantly different from the morphology of pure P5FIn (Fig. 1C). This fibrous structure can significantly improve the electron transfer rate and ion exchange rate, and can also increase the specific surface area of P5FIn/Au nanocomposite to enhance the load of TBA. Fig. 1D was the UV–vis absorption spectra of Au (a), P5FIn (b) and P5FIn/Au(c) nanocomposite. As shown in curve a, Au had a broad absorption peak at 650 nm, which demonstrated that Au had good absorption of visible light. From the curve b, it can be seen that the main absorption band of pure P5FIn caused by π-π* transition was about 380–400 nm. When Au and P5FIn were combined, two absorption peaks were observed (curve c), and the absorption peak intensity was stronger and wider than before. The reason was that Au particles were doped into the backbone of the polymer, resulting in the change of structure and morphology of polymer, and improving the light absorption and light utilization of the complex [27]. The absorption range of light extended to the visible region with formation of nanocomposite, which was beneficial to improve the PEC performance of P5FIn/Au nanocomposite.

In order to explore the photoelectric properties of P5FIn/Au nanocomposite, oxygen (O2) was used as the electron-hole scavenger in the PBS system for suppressing the recombination of electron-hole pairs and enhancing its separation efficiency. Fig. 2 A was the photocurrent response of bare ITO (a), pure P5FIn (b) and P5FIn/Au nanocomposite (c). The photocurrent response of the P5FIn/Au nanocomposite was 3.9 times higher than the pure P5FIn because the Au with high conductivity was embedded into P5FIn. As the signal enhancer, Au can improve the optical properties of P5FIn and enhance the ability of transport electrons, and thus effectively suppress the recombination of electron-hole pair. In PEC sensors, efficiency of photoelectric conversion and photocurrent intensity of photoelectric materials had a great influence on the sensitivity of the sensors. Therefore, the introduction of Au into the P5FIn was very advantageous for the PEC properties of the nanocomposite material. Based on the above advantages, P5FIn/Au nanocomposite had a great application prospect in the field of PEC sensing.

To better understand the mechanism of electron transfer about P5FIn/Au nanocomposite, the energy level of P5FIn/Au nanocomposite was calculated by UV–vis absorption spectra [28,29]. The cathode photocurrent generation mechanism of P5FIn/Au/ITO modified electrode was described in Fig. 2B. Firstly, the P5FIn/Au nanocomposite absorbed light energy from ground state to excited state, and photogenerated electrons were transferred from the HOMO level to the LUMO level to form a photoelectron-hole pair. Au can accelerate the transfer of electrons and improve the migration of photo-generated electrons. Then, the photo-generated electrons were transferred from the LUMO energy level to the electron acceptor (O2) in the electrolyte. The electrons of Au migrated to the surface of Au, and these electrons entered into the photo-generated holes of P5FIn. Finally, the ITO electrode provided electrons to return the material to its ground state. At this point, the photocurrent generation cycle was completed and the photocurrent was formed. After the specific recognition of TB and TBA, the immune complexes formed by TB and TBA hindered electron transfer on the electrode surface. As the concentration of TB increased, the intensity of the photocurrent gradually decreased. Thus, TB can be quantitatively detected in this manner.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was an effective method for characterizing the interfacial properties of electrodes and the construction process of PEC sensor [30]. After TB and TBA were immunoreacted, the immune complex was formed on the electrode surface which acted as an inert blocking layer to hinder electron transfer. Fig. 2C showed the EIS Nyquist diagram for each step of the electrode modification process. In the EIS measurement, [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− was used as the redox probe and the frequency was set in the range of 0.1 Hz–10,000 Hz. As shown in curve a, the blank ITO electrode showed a very small semicircle diameter, which meant that the ITO electrode had a high conductivity (curve a). When P5FIn/Au was modified on the surface of the electrode, the semicircle diameter increased because the P5FIn/Au nanocomposite (curve b) was an organic semiconductor material that can inhibit the electron transfer of the [Fe(CN)6]3/4− probe. Then, after the TBA was modified on the surface of the electrode (curve c), the semicircle diameter was further increased due to the insulating properties of DNA. When BSA (curve d) and TB (curve e, c = 10 nM) were gradually modified on the surface of electrode, the diameter of the semicircle was further increased due to the insulating properties of protein. Therefore, the EIS diagram demonstrated that the PEC aptasensor was successfully constructed.

The PEC aptasensor constructed process can also be characterized by the photocurrent response of the modified electrode. As shown in Fig. 2D, there was no obvious photocurrent signal of bare ITO (curve a). The P5FIn/Au nanocomposite modified ITO electrode (curve b) exhibited a strong photocurrent signal, which was ascribed to the good PEC properties of this nanocomposite. Subsequently, after the amino-functionalized TBA chain (curve c) was modified on the surface of the P5FIn/Au/ITO electrode, the photocurrent intensity was reduced due to the inhibition of electron transfer by the DNA strand. When BSA were modified onto the electrode surface (curve d), the photocurrent intensity further reduced. BSA is a non-conducting protein molecule which can block the electron transfer. Finally, as shown in curve e, after TB (c = 10 nM) was bounded to TBA specifically, the photocurrent signal was further reduced due to the steric hindrance of the protein. The experimental results also showed that the PEC aptasensor was successfully fabricated.

To achieve optimal analytical performance, two important parameters affecting the PEC aptasensor were studied: incubation time and solution pH. As shown in Fig. S1A, the optimal incubation time for the experiment was 70 min and the optimum pH was 7.4. The details of the experiment can be found in Supporting information.

When TB was modified to the surface of modified electrode, it blocked the electron transport, resulting in the decrease of photocurrent. Under the above optimized conditions, this prepared PEC aptasensor was used to quantitatively detect TB. Fig. 3 A showed the photocurrent intensity corresponding to different concentrations of TB, in which the photocurrent intensity decreased with the increase of TB concentration. As shown in Fig. 3B, the change amount of photocurrent (ΔI=|Ioff- Ion|) had a good linear relationship with the logarithm of TB concentration, and its linear range was 0.001–10 nM. The linear correlation equation of the calibration curve was ΔI = 4.2649–0.6748cthrombin, and the linear correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9923. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.2 pM after calculated by 3σ method.

Part previous reported literatures for detecting TB were listed in Table 1 . As shown in Table 1, compared with other methods of detecting TB, such as MESA [31], ECL [32], fluorescence method [33], this sensor based on P5FIn/Au nanocomposite has relatively wider detection range (0.001–10 nM) and lower detection limit (0.2 pM). Compared with the reported PEC sensor based on Graphene–CdS [12], the construction process of this sensor is also simpler and faster. The main reasons for this good performance were as follows. Firstly, P5FIn/Au nanocomposite can generate more photogenerated electron-hole pairs and produce high photocurrent intensity, which effectively improves the sensitivity of the sensor. Secondly, Au with good electronic transfer capability can inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, resulting in enhanced photocurrent signals. Thirdly, P5FIn/Au nanocomposite had large specific interface area with aldehyde groups, which was beneficial to increase the loading of biomolecules. The excellent detection capability of the PEC sensor is important to the practical application of the sensor, and simple and effective construction process of this PEC sensor also provides new idea for the next development of the PEC sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reproducibility and selectivity of the PEC aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of this PEC aptasensor was assessed by detecting the photocurrent response of 0.1 nM TB solution. Fig. 4 A showed the photocurrent response curve of the sensor after continuing 19th ON/OFF cycle of light source. It can be seen that the photocurrent of the aptasensor had no significant change after a long period of circulation, indicating the good stability of aptasensor. In addition, after the sensor was placed at 4 °C for 15 days, the photocurrent response value still maintained 92% of its initial value (Fig. 4B), suggesting the good storage stability of this aptasensor.

The reproducibility of this PEC aptasensor was investigated by the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the test results. In same measurement conditions, using two sets (5/set) of electrodes in the same batch to determine the repeatability of the sensor at different TB concentrations (0.01 nM and 1 nM, respectively). It can be seen from the Fig. 4C that the photocurrent was not much different with the same TB concentration. The RSDs were 3.0163% (0.01 nM TB) and 2.7091% (1 nM TB) toward the two samples, respectively. In order to investigate whether the electrodes of different manufacturers and batches have an effect on the experimental results, then in same measurement conditions, two sets (5/set) of electrodes in different batches were used to detect the corresponding concentrations of TB (0.01 nM and 1 nM, respectively) (Fig. 4C). The RSDs of the measurement results were 3.9294% (0.01 nM TB) and 3.6431% (1 nM TB), respectively. The above results showed that this aptasensor had good reproducibility and repeatability.

Non-specific adsorption can affect the accuracy and sensitivity detection of sensor. Thus, selectivity is an important indicator to evaluate the performance of sensor. The modified electrode was incubated with different interfering substances to explore the selectivity of this aptasensor. Fig. 4D displayed the photocurrent of this aptasensor in blank sample (a), CEA (b), HIgG (c), glucose (d), TB (0.1 nM) (e) and the mixture of these substance (f). The concentrations of the above interferences were 10 nM. As can be seen from Fig. 4D, the photocurrent signals detected by the PEC aptasensor of these interfering substances (including CEA, HIgG and glucose) were not significantly different from the photocurrent of the blank sample. In the case of mixing the interfering substances with TB, there was no significant difference of photocurrent signal compared with the TB sample. These experimental results indicated the good selectivity of this sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Actual samples detection of PEC aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitive and rapid determination of TB in real samples is important for the application of the sensor. Thus, TB samples in human serum were analyzed by standard addition methods. Human serum samples were diluted with PBS (pH 7.4), then TB was added to configure a series of standard samples. This serum samples were then detected by the constructed aptasensor. The experimental results were showed in Table 2 . In human serum samples, the recovery of TB detection was 96.7%-107.3%, and the RSD was less than 5% (1.2%–5.0%). This experimental date proved that the aptasensor can be used for the detection of actual samples.

In summary, a simple label-free PEC aptasensor was successfully constructed based on P5FIn/Au nanocomposite for detection of TB with high sensitivity and selectivity. As PEC matrix interfaces of the sensing electrode, P5FIn/Au nanocomposite exhibits excellent PEC performance and effectively improves the detection capability of the sensor. As the signal enhancer, Au can obviously promote the photocurrent response due to it inhibits the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs of P5FIn. This PEC aptasensor showed good analytical results with low detection limits, good stability and reproducibility. Moreover, the exact diagnoses for human serum samples were achieved, which indicated a promising application prospect of this PEC sensor for bioanalysis and diagnosis.

Chaonan Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Zhiyuan Li: Investigation, Formal analysis. Ya Tian: Data curation. Qingfu Guo: Writing - review & editing. Guangming Nie: Supervision, Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51973102), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (ZR2019MB067) and Talent Fund of QUST (2019).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The materials, reagents, and apparatus utilized in this work. The preparation process of P5FIn/Au nanocomposite. Influence of different reaction parameters on the photocurrent response (Fig. S1). Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105452.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
